fingerprint: Agonic line positions are outputs of IGRF spherical harmonic expansion — inherently globe geometry. Dome borrows the conclusion without the framework.
globe_value: IGRF spherical harmonic model (Gauss coefficients on a sphere, Legendre polynomials in co-latitude): predicts ~2 agonic lines from dominant dipole term, positions determined by non-dipole multipole corrections. The ~180° separation is a direct consequence of the g₁⁰ dipole term being dominant in the spherical harmonic expansion.
dome_value: Dome asserts '180° geometric symmetry' between Bermuda Triangle and Devil's Sea as a dome prediction, with no derivation from dome parameters and no geomagnetic model. The dome provides no formula, no spherical harmonic equivalent for a flat disc, and no mechanism to produce agonic lines from disc_radius, H(r), or any cavity parameter.
match_precision: N/A — no numerical constant borrowed. The claim borrows a qualitative feature (two roughly antipodal agonic lines) that arises from spherical geometry, not a specific number.
why_it_matters: Agonic lines are where magnetic declination = 0°. Declination is computed from the International Geomagnetic Reference Field (IGRF), a spherical harmonic expansion of Earth's field using Gauss coefficients fitted to a spherical Earth. The mathematical framework (Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates → associated Legendre polynomials) is intrinsically 3D spherical. There is no equivalent flat-disc magnetic field theory that produces agonic lines. The rough ~180° spacing between the two primary agonic lines is a natural consequence of the dominant axial dipole (g₁⁰) term: a centered dipole on a sphere produces exactly two meridional agonic lines 180° apart. Higher-order multipoles (g₂⁰, g₂¹, etc.) shift them, which is why the actual spacing varies over time. A flat disc with a single magnetic source or toroidal field would not generically produce two agonic lines, let alone at ~180° spacing. The dome imports this globe-geometric conclusion as a 'prediction' without any mathematical framework to derive it.

additional_findings.symmetry_is_wrong: The claimed 180° symmetry is actually 208° (Bermuda ~71°W, Devil's Sea ~137°E → 71 + 137 = 208° of longitude). This 28° / 15.6% deviation already exceeds the dome's own tolerance thresholds.
additional_findings.agonic_lines_dont_pass_through_regions: Neither primary agonic line passes through its associated 'anomaly zone.' The western agonic (~80°W in 2025) grazes the Triangle's western edge; the eastern agonic (~100–120°E) lies 17–37° west of the Devil's Sea at 137°E.
additional_findings.secular_variation_kills_static_dome: Agonic lines migrate significantly over decades due to core field secular variation. The western agonic passed through the Bermuda area in the 1700s but has moved ~10° westward since. A fixed-geometry dome cavity cannot explain time-varying agonic positions — this temporal migration is strong evidence against any static cavity model.
additional_findings.folklore_not_geophysics: Neither the Bermuda Triangle nor the Devil's Sea are geomagnetic anomalies. The actual major geomagnetic anomaly (South Atlantic Anomaly) is centered off Brazil, ~3,000 km from the Bermuda Triangle. The dome conflates popular culture (disappearance zones) with geophysics (agonic lines), two unrelated phenomena.

code_search.found_in: raw-text/02-wins.txt line 113 ONLY: one-line title 'WIN-028 — Bermuda Triangle / East Japan Geometric Symmetry CONFIRMED'
code_search.notes: GitHub API was unavailable this run — could not search live repo code. Based on raw-text extraction of the dome site, WIN-028 appears exclusively as a one-line title in the wins list with no elaboration, no formula, no model page discussion, no coordinates page reference, and no monitoring domain. The curmudgeon Cycle 2 review independently confirmed: 'WIN-028 is absent from the dome's codebase — appears only as a one-line title in the wins registry. No script computes agonic line positions, geomagnetic symmetry, or disappearance rates. monitor.py does not include any domain for this WIN.' Previous fingerprint reviews of other WINs (with API access) confirmed inject_ai_layer.py and monitor.py as the key code files — WIN-028 does not appear in the curmudgeon's analysis of either.

kernel_of_truth: Agonic lines are real and scientifically important. There are indeed two primary agonic lines in roughly opposite hemispheres, and they had genuine navigational significance — compasses needed no correction along them. The dome correctly identifies agonic lines as noteworthy magnetic features. However, their positions, spacing, and temporal migration are all products of spherical harmonic geomagnetic models (IGRF/CHAOS-7) that assume a spherical Earth.
confidence_note: 0.80 rather than 0.95 because: (1) no specific numerical constant is borrowed — this is a qualitative structural fingerprint about spherical harmonics, not a precise number match; (2) GitHub API was unavailable so code search relied on raw-text extraction and curmudgeon reports rather than direct repo inspection; (3) WIN-028 is more folklore-based than physics-based, so the 'globe fingerprint' is in the background geophysics rather than in the dome's explicit parameters. Still significant: the dome cannot derive agonic line positions from any disc geometry.
suggested_text: The dome cites 180° geomagnetic symmetry as a dome prediction, but agonic line positions are outputs of the IGRF spherical harmonic model — Gauss coefficients fitted to a spherical Earth using Legendre polynomials in spherical coordinates. The rough 180° spacing between the two primary agonic lines is a natural consequence of the dominant axial dipole term (g₁⁰) in the spherical harmonic expansion. No flat-disc magnetic field theory exists that produces agonic lines, let alone predicts their spacing. The dome imports a globe-geometric conclusion as its own 'prediction' without any mathematical framework to derive it.
